title: S1 S1 May2023 Q1 — Histogram
meta: S1 · Q1 · S1 May 2023 (WST01/01, Thu 11 May 2023) · Attempted 12 May 2026
heading: Q1 — Histogram
question_html:
The histogram shows the distances, in km, that 274 people travel to work. Given that 60 of these people travel between 10 km and 20 km to work, estimate
(a) the number of people who travel between 22 km and 45 km to work, (3)
(b) the median distance travelled to work by these 274 people, (2)
(c) the mean distance travelled to work by these 274 people. (3)

Histogram readings (calibrated against the named class): since 60 people lie in $[10,20)$ and the class width is 10, $\text{FD}_{[10,20)} = 6$. Reading the remaining bars against this scale:

Class (km) Width FD Frequency $f$ Midpoint
$[0,10)$ 10 0.5 5 5
$[10,20)$ 10 6 60 15
$[20,30)$ 10 7 70 25
$[30,40)$ 10 10 100 35
$[40,50)$ 10 3 30 45
$[50,60)$ 10 0.9 9 55
Total 274

Check: $5+60+70+100+30+9 = 274$ ✓

steps.label: Work block 1
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Work block 1. The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(a) People in $[22, 45)$: slice across three bars.

Mistake guard from the attempt/revision notes.

What went wrong (potential pitfalls noted from img_00.png).. Phuc began part (b) by writing "$274/2 = 137$" and "$274 - 60 = 214$", indicating an attempt to subtract bar frequencies sequentially rather than build a cumulative-frequency column. Risk: losing track of which class contains the 137th value.

Phuc's incorrect approach (reconstructed for (a)).. A common error is to ignore the partial-bar pro-rating and treat $[20,40)$ as a clean two-class slice, then subtract a guess for the $[40,45)$ slice --- e.g. computing $70 + 100 + 30 = 200$ for $[20,50)$ and trying to remove "the bit between 20 and 22" by eye. This conflates full bars and partial bars and loses 2 marks in (a).

From the Loom.. The Loom for this sitting focuses on a different paper's whiteboard work --- there is no verbatim Mr. Fuat quote on Q1. The principle Mr. Fuat consistently states across S1 sittings is: "Calibrate first, then never read FD off the axis again --- read off your scale."

steps.label: Work block 2
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Work block 2. The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

  • $[22,30)$: width 8, FD 7 $\Rightarrow$ $f = 8 \times 7 = 56$
  • $[30,40)$: full, $f = 100$
  • $[40,45)$: width 5, FD 3 $\Rightarrow$ $f = 5 \times 3 = 15$

steps.label: Work block 3
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Work block 3. The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

$$\text{Total} = 56 + 100 + 15 = \boxed{171}.$$

steps.label: Work block 4
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Work block 4. The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(b) Median. Position $= n/2 = 137$. Cumulative frequencies: $5, 65, 135, 235, \ldots$ The 137th value lies in $[30,40)$ with $L=30$, $F=135$, $f_m=100$, $w=10$:

steps.label: Work block 5
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Work block 5. The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

$$\text{median} = 30 + \frac{137-135}{100}\times 10 = 30 + 0.2 = \boxed{30.2 \text{ km}}.$$

steps.label: Work block 6
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Work block 6. The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(c) Mean.
$$\sum f x_{\text{mid}} = 5(5)+60(15)+70(25)+100(35)+30(45)+9(55)$$
$$= 25 + 900 + 1750 + 3500 + 1350 + 495 = 8020.$$
$$\bar{x} = \frac{8020}{274} = 29.27\ldots \approx \boxed{29.3 \text{ km (3 s.f.)}}.$$

steps.label: Audit checklist
steps.body:
Strategy. Use this final box after solving to check formula choice, mark-winning statements, units, and the specific traps from this paper.

Active-recall protocol. Before revealing any work box, say out loud what the visible question is asking for, list the given values with units, and choose the formula or theorem that earns the method mark. Then reveal one box at a time and check that your own line of working has the same structure: setup first, substitution second, simplification third, boxed answer last. If a part depends on a previous result, write that carried result at the top of your scratch work before starting the new part. After the final box, check rounding, units, sign, domain restrictions, and whether the answer is plausible in the context of the question. This card is built so the question header remains visible while every grey work box can be masked separately in Anki Image Occlusion.

Self-test prompts. Can you name the first formula without looking? Can you explain why the denominator, sign, or transformation is the one used here? Can you reproduce the algebra without skipping the intermediate line that would earn the method mark? Can you state the final answer with the exact rounding and context the examiner expects? If any answer is no, re-mask the relevant work box and repeat that part before moving to the next question.

Formula anchor.

Key formula / concept.
$$f = \text{FD}\times w,\qquad \text{median} = L + \frac{n/2 - F}{f_m}\,w,\qquad \bar x = \frac{\sum f x_{\text{mid}}}{\sum f}.$$

Exam checklist before moving on.

Exam checklist.
- Calibration computed before reading other bars.
- For partial-class queries: every shaded rectangle = FD $\times$ slice width.
- Cumulative frequency column written down before locating the median class.
- Midpoints (not boundaries) used for the mean.
- Final answers to 3 s.f. with units (km).

Common traps to actively avoid.

Common pitfalls.
- Reading FD straight off the y-axis without doing the calibration check.
- Pro-rating but using the wrong sub-width (e.g. 7 instead of 8 for $[22,30)$).
- Using $(n+1)/2$ for the median position (that's discrete data --- for continuous grouped data it is $n/2$).
- Treating "between 22 and 45" as inclusive of full bars only.
steps.result: Completion target: answer matches the boxed result and every checklist item is satisfied.

title: S1 S1 May2023 Q2 — Bivariate Data, Regression, Coding
meta: S1 · Q2 · S1 May 2023 (WST01/01, Thu 11 May 2023) · Attempted 12 May 2026
heading: Q2 — Bivariate Data, Regression, Coding
question_html:
Two students, Olive and Shan, collect data on the weight, $w$ grams, and the tail length, $t$ cm, of 15 mice. Olive summarised the data as follows:
$$S_{tt} = 5.3173,\ \ \sum w^2 = 6089.12,\ \ \sum tw = 2304.53,\ \ \sum w = 297.8,\ \ \sum t = 114.8.$$
(a) Calculate the value of $S_{tw}$ and the value of $S_{ww}$. (3)
(b) Calculate the value of the product moment correlation coefficient between $w$ and $t$. (2)
(c) Show that the equation of the regression line of $w$ on $t$ can be written as $w = -16.7 + 4.77t$. (3)
(d) Give an interpretation of the gradient of the regression line. (1)
(e) Explain why it would not be appropriate to use the regression line in part (c) to estimate the weight of a mouse with a tail length of 2 cm. (2)
Shan decided to code the data using $x = t - 6$ and $y = \frac{w}{2} - 5$.
(f) Write down the value of the product moment correlation coefficient between $x$ and $y$. (1)
(g) Write down an equation of the regression line of $y$ on $x$. You do not need to simplify your equation. (1)

steps.label: Part (a)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (a). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(a)
$$S_{tw} = \sum tw - \frac{\sum t \sum w}{n} = 2304.53 - \frac{114.8 \times 297.8}{15}.$$
$$\frac{114.8 \times 297.8}{15} = \frac{34187.44}{15} = 2279.16267.$$
$$S_{tw} = 2304.53 - 2279.16267 = 25.3673\overline{3} \approx \boxed{25.4}.$$

$$S_{ww} = \sum w^2 - \frac{(\sum w)^2}{n} = 6089.12 - \frac{297.8^2}{15} = 6089.12 - 5912.32267 = 176.79733 \approx \boxed{177}.$$

Mistake guard from the attempt/revision notes.

What went wrong (anticipated).. The most frequent S1 errors at this sub-question type:
- Sign omission in the $S_{tw}$ formula (writing $\sum tw + \frac{\sum t \sum w}{n}$).
- Premature rounding so the printed "show that" coefficients fail to match.
- Recomputing $S_{xy}, S_{xx}$ from coded data in (g) instead of substituting the coding into the original line.
- Forgetting that PMCC is invariant under positive linear coding.

Phuc's likely incorrect approach (g).. Treating part (g) as if it required new $S_{xy}$/$S_{xx}$ calculations, rather than substituting $t = x+6$ and $w = 2(y+5)$ into the line from (c).

steps.label: Part (b)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (b). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(b)
$$r = \frac{S_{tw}}{\sqrt{S_{tt} S_{ww}}} = \frac{25.36733}{\sqrt{5.3173 \times 176.79733}} = \frac{25.36733}{\sqrt{940.0457}} = \frac{25.36733}{30.6608} = 0.82734 \approx \boxed{0.827}.$$

steps.label: Part (c)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (c). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(c) Means:
$$\bar t = 114.8/15 = 7.65333,\qquad \bar w = 297.8/15 = 19.85333.$$
Gradient:
$$b = \frac{S_{tw}}{S_{tt}} = \frac{25.36733}{5.3173} = 4.77073 \approx 4.77.$$
Intercept:
$$a = \bar w - b\bar t = 19.85333 - 4.77073 \times 7.65333 = 19.85333 - 36.51324 = -16.65991 \approx -16.7.$$
Hence $w = -16.7 + 4.77t$. ✓

steps.label: Part (d)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (d). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(d) "For each additional 1 cm of tail length, the weight increases by approximately 4.77 g."

steps.label: Part (e)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (e). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(e) $\bar t = 7.65$ cm with $S_{tt} = 5.3173$ $\Rightarrow$ $s_t \approx \sqrt{5.3173/14} \approx 0.62$ cm; the data lie roughly in 6.4--8.9 cm. A tail length of 2 cm is far outside this range --- using the regression line at $t=2$ is extrapolation, and the prediction $w = -16.7 + 4.77(2) = -7.16$ g is physically impossible (negative weight), confirming the model is invalid there.

steps.label: Part (f)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (f). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(f) Linear coding with positive scale factors preserves $r$. Both $t \mapsto x = t-6$ (scale $+1$) and $w \mapsto y = w/2 - 5$ (scale $+1/2$) are positive-linear, so:
$$r_{xy} = r_{tw} = \boxed{0.827}.$$

steps.label: Part (g)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (g). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(g) Substitute $t = x+6$, $w = 2y + 10$ into $w = -16.7 + 4.77t$:
$$2y + 10 = -16.7 + 4.77(x+6).$$

The question explicitly allows the unsimplified form, so this answer is acceptable. (For reference: simplifying gives $y = 2.385x + 0.96$.)

steps.label: Audit checklist
steps.body:
Strategy. Use this final box after solving to check formula choice, mark-winning statements, units, and the specific traps from this paper.

Active-recall protocol. Before revealing any work box, say out loud what the visible question is asking for, list the given values with units, and choose the formula or theorem that earns the method mark. Then reveal one box at a time and check that your own line of working has the same structure: setup first, substitution second, simplification third, boxed answer last. If a part depends on a previous result, write that carried result at the top of your scratch work before starting the new part. After the final box, check rounding, units, sign, domain restrictions, and whether the answer is plausible in the context of the question. This card is built so the question header remains visible while every grey work box can be masked separately in Anki Image Occlusion.

Self-test prompts. Can you name the first formula without looking? Can you explain why the denominator, sign, or transformation is the one used here? Can you reproduce the algebra without skipping the intermediate line that would earn the method mark? Can you state the final answer with the exact rounding and context the examiner expects? If any answer is no, re-mask the relevant work box and repeat that part before moving to the next question.

Formula anchor.

Key formula / concept.
$$S_{xy} = \sum xy - \frac{\sum x \sum y}{n},\quad r = \frac{S_{xy}}{\sqrt{S_{xx}S_{yy}}},\quad y = a+bx \text{ with } b = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}},\ a = \bar y - b\bar x.$$
Coding: $r$ invariant under positive linear coding; for the regression line, substitute the coding into the existing equation rather than recomputing from scratch.

Exam checklist before moving on.

Exam checklist.
- Carry $\geq$ 5 s.f. through all intermediate $S_{xy}$, $b$, $a$ working.
- For "show that" with printed answers, write $b$ and $a$ to $\geq$ 4 s.f. before rounding.
- Interpretation in (d) uses units (g per cm).
- For (e), state both outside the range and (ideally) the impossible prediction value.
- For (g), use the substitution shortcut --- do not redo PMCC working.

Common traps to actively avoid.

Common pitfalls.
- Sign error: $S_{tw} = \sum tw - (\sum t)(\sum w)/n$ --- subtract.
- Quoting $r$ without checking $-1 \le r \le 1$.
- Confusing "$w$ on $t$" with "$t$ on $w$": $w$ on $t$ uses $b = S_{tw}/S_{tt}$ (denominator is the explanatory variable).
- Forgetting that the PMCC is dimensionless and would still equal 0.827 even if all weights were doubled (positive scaling).
steps.result: Completion target: answer matches the boxed result and every checklist item is satisfied.

title: S1 S1 May2023 Q3 — Coded Mean / Variance, Box Plot, Outliers
meta: S1 · Q3 · S1 May 2023 (WST01/01, Thu 11 May 2023) · Attempted 12 May 2026
heading: Q3 — Coded Mean / Variance, Box Plot, Outliers
question_html:
Jim records the length, $l$ mm, of 81 salmon. The data are coded using $x = l - 600$ with summary $\sum x = 3711$, $\sum x^2 = 475181$.
(a) Find the mean length of these salmon. (3)
(b) Find the variance of the lengths of these salmon. (2)
The weight, $w$ grams, of each of the 81 salmon is recorded to the nearest gram and summarised in the box plot with $Q_1 = 3000$, median $= 4000$, $Q_3 = 5500$, min $= 2000$, max $= 7700$.
(c) Find the maximum number of salmon that have weights in the interval $4600 < w \le 7700$. (1)
(d) Raj says that the box plot is incorrect as Jim has not included outliers. For these data an outlier is a value more than $1.5 \times \mathrm{IQR}$ above $Q_3$ or below $Q_1$. Show that there are no outliers. (3)

steps.label: Part (a)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (a). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(a)
$$\bar x = \frac{\sum x}{n} = \frac{3711}{81} = 45.8148.$$
Reverse the shift:
$$\bar l = \bar x + 600 = 645.8148 \approx \boxed{645.8 \text{ mm}}.$$

Mistake guard from the attempt/revision notes.

What went wrong.. Two recurring mistakes in this question family:
1. Reverse-coding the variance ("$\mathrm{Var}(l) = \mathrm{Var}(x) - 600$" --- a non-sequitur; variance is invariant under translation).
2. In (c), computing $(n - \text{position of }Q_2)/2$ rather than reasoning about how many values can lie strictly above the median.

Phuc's likely incorrect approach (c).. Computing "$Q_3 - 4600 = 900$, scaled by something" --- trying to interpolate inside the box. This question is a concept question, not an interpolation question.

steps.label: Part (b)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (b). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(b) Variance is invariant under shift, so $\mathrm{Var}(l) = \mathrm{Var}(x)$:
$$\mathrm{Var}(x) = \frac{\sum x^2}{n} - \bar x^2 = \frac{475181}{81} - (45.8148)^2 = 5866.4321 - 2098.9966 = 3767.4355 \approx \boxed{3770 \text{ (3 s.f.)}}.$$

steps.label: Part (c)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (c). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(c) The interval is $4600 < w \le 7700$. Since $4600 > \text{median} = 4000$, every value in the interval lies strictly above the median. By definition of the median (with $n=81$), at most $\lfloor 81/2 \rfloor = 40$ data points can lie strictly above 4000. Therefore the maximum count in $(4600, 7700]$ is $\boxed{40}$.

steps.label: Part (d)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (d). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(d) IQR = $5500 - 3000 = 2500$.
- Lower fence: $Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \mathrm{IQR} = 3000 - 3750 = -750$.
- Upper fence: $Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \mathrm{IQR} = 5500 + 3750 = 9250$.

Min $= 2000 > -750$ ✓ and Max $= 7700 < 9250$ ✓ --- neither extreme exceeds either fence, so no outliers exist. ✓

steps.label: Audit checklist
steps.body:
Strategy. Use this final box after solving to check formula choice, mark-winning statements, units, and the specific traps from this paper.

Active-recall protocol. Before revealing any work box, say out loud what the visible question is asking for, list the given values with units, and choose the formula or theorem that earns the method mark. Then reveal one box at a time and check that your own line of working has the same structure: setup first, substitution second, simplification third, boxed answer last. If a part depends on a previous result, write that carried result at the top of your scratch work before starting the new part. After the final box, check rounding, units, sign, domain restrictions, and whether the answer is plausible in the context of the question. This card is built so the question header remains visible while every grey work box can be masked separately in Anki Image Occlusion.

Self-test prompts. Can you name the first formula without looking? Can you explain why the denominator, sign, or transformation is the one used here? Can you reproduce the algebra without skipping the intermediate line that would earn the method mark? Can you state the final answer with the exact rounding and context the examiner expects? If any answer is no, re-mask the relevant work box and repeat that part before moving to the next question.

Formula anchor.

Key formula / concept.
$$\mathrm{Var}(x+a) = \mathrm{Var}(x),\quad \mathrm{Var}(bx) = b^2 \mathrm{Var}(x),\quad \mathrm{Var}(x) = \frac{\sum x^2}{n} - \bar x^2.$$
Outlier rule: outside $[Q_1 - 1.5\mathrm{IQR},\, Q_3 + 1.5\mathrm{IQR}]$.

Exam checklist before moving on.

Exam checklist.
- Mean reverse-coded with the shift constant.
- Variance left untouched (no $\pm 600$).
- Both fences computed and both extremes compared.
- For "max number above bound" questions, compare the bound to median/quartiles before answering.

Common traps to actively avoid.

Common pitfalls.
- Adding 600 to the variance.
- Quoting variance with units squared but no number agreement (always include units mm$^2$ where possible).
- Forgetting that the upper fence still has to be checked even when intuition says the data look fine.
- Using $(n+1)/2$ for the median's positional logic in (c).
steps.result: Completion target: answer matches the boxed result and every checklist item is satisfied.

title: S1 S1 May2023 Q4 — Tree, Total Probability, Bayes
meta: S1 · Q4 · S1 May 2023 (WST01/01, Thu 11 May 2023) · Attempted 12 May 2026
heading: Q4 — Tree, Total Probability, Bayes
question_html:
A bag contains a large number of coloured counters. Each counter is labelled A, B or C: 30% A, 45% B, the rest C. Of those labelled A, 2% are red; of those labelled B, 4% are red; of those labelled C, 6% are red. One counter is selected at random.
(a) Complete the tree diagram. (2)
(b) Find $P(\text{labelled A and not red})$. (2)
(c) Find $P(\text{red})$. (2)
(d) Given the counter is red, find $P(\text{labelled C})$. (3)

steps.label: Setup
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Setup. The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

Setup: $P(A)=0.30$, $P(B)=0.45$, $P(C)=1-0.30-0.45=0.25$. Conditional reds: $0.02, 0.04, 0.06$. Not-reds: $0.98, 0.96, 0.94$.

Mistake guard from the attempt/revision notes.

What went wrong.. Bayes-style questions trip students on direction: $P(C \mid R)$ and $P(R \mid C)$ are not interchangeable.

Phuc's likely incorrect approach (d).. Quoting $P(R \mid C) = 0.06$ as the answer.

steps.label: Part (b)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (b). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(b)
$$P(A \cap \text{not red}) = 0.30 \times 0.98 = \boxed{0.294}.$$

(c) Total probability:
$$P(R) = 0.30(0.02) + 0.45(0.04) + 0.25(0.06) = 0.006 + 0.018 + 0.015 = \boxed{0.039}.$$

(d) Bayes:
$$P(C \mid R) = \frac{P(C)\,P(R \mid C)}{P(R)} = \frac{0.25 \times 0.06}{0.039} = \frac{0.015}{0.039} = \frac{15}{39} = \frac{5}{13} \approx \boxed{0.385}.$$

steps.label: Audit checklist
steps.body:
Strategy. Use this final box after solving to check formula choice, mark-winning statements, units, and the specific traps from this paper.

Active-recall protocol. Before revealing any work box, say out loud what the visible question is asking for, list the given values with units, and choose the formula or theorem that earns the method mark. Then reveal one box at a time and check that your own line of working has the same structure: setup first, substitution second, simplification third, boxed answer last. If a part depends on a previous result, write that carried result at the top of your scratch work before starting the new part. After the final box, check rounding, units, sign, domain restrictions, and whether the answer is plausible in the context of the question. This card is built so the question header remains visible while every grey work box can be masked separately in Anki Image Occlusion.

Self-test prompts. Can you name the first formula without looking? Can you explain why the denominator, sign, or transformation is the one used here? Can you reproduce the algebra without skipping the intermediate line that would earn the method mark? Can you state the final answer with the exact rounding and context the examiner expects? If any answer is no, re-mask the relevant work box and repeat that part before moving to the next question.

Formula anchor.

Key formula / concept.
$$P(R) = \sum_i P(L_i)\,P(R \mid L_i),\qquad P(L_i \mid R) = \frac{P(L_i)\,P(R \mid L_i)}{P(R)}.$$

Exam checklist before moving on.

Exam checklist.
- Tree diagram drawn with all six branches and all probabilities labelled.
- "The rest" probability for C computed and stated.
- Bayes formula written before substitution.
- Final answer to 3 s.f. (or as a clean fraction $5/13$).

Common traps to actively avoid.

Common pitfalls.
- Forgetting the third row of the tree (computing only A and B contributions).
- Inverting Bayes (giving 0.06 instead of 0.385).
- Decimal slip in $0.25 \times 0.06 = 0.015$.
steps.result: Completion target: answer matches the boxed result and every checklist item is satisfied.

title: S1 S1 May2023 Q5 — Discrete Random Variable, Transformation
meta: S1 · Q5 · S1 May 2023 (WST01/01, Thu 11 May 2023) · Attempted 12 May 2026
heading: Q5 — Discrete Random Variable, Transformation
question_html:
A discrete RV $Y$ has pf
$$P(Y=y) = \begin{cases} k(3-y) & y=1,2 \ k(y^2 - 8) & y=3,4,5 \ k & y=6 \ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
(a) Show that $k = 1/30$. (2) Find:
(b) $P(1 < Y \le 4)$. (2)
(c) $E(Y)$. (2)
The random variable $X = 15 - 2Y$.
(d) Calculate $P(Y \le X)$. (3)
(e) Calculate $\mathrm{Var}(X)$. (4)

steps.label: Setup
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Setup. The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

Tabulate the pmf in $k$:

$y$ 1 2 3 4 5 6
$P(Y=y)$ $2k$ $k$ $k$ $8k$ $17k$ $k$

(a) Sum to 1:
$$2k + k + k + 8k + 17k + k = 30k = 1 \implies k = \frac{1}{30}. \quad ✓$$

Numerical pmf: $P(Y=y) = \tfrac{2}{30}, \tfrac{1}{30}, \tfrac{1}{30}, \tfrac{8}{30}, \tfrac{17}{30}, \tfrac{1}{30}$ for $y=1\ldots 6$.

Mistake guard from the attempt/revision notes.

What went wrong.. Three failure modes:
1. Sign error in $k(3-y)$ (writing $k(y-3)$, giving negative probabilities).
2. In (d), interpreting "$Y \le X$" with $X$ as if it were independent of $Y$ instead of the deterministic function $X = 15 - 2Y$.
3. In (e), computing $\mathrm{Var}(15 - 2Y) = -2\,\mathrm{Var}(Y)$ or $2\,\mathrm{Var}(Y)$, missing the $a^2$.

Phuc's likely incorrect approach (d).. Trying to enumerate joint $(Y, X)$ pairs and summing $P(Y=y)P(X=x)$ --- but $X$ and $Y$ are perfectly correlated (one determines the other). Solve algebraically instead.

steps.label: Part (b)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (b). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(b)
$$P(1 < Y \le 4) = P(Y=2)+P(Y=3)+P(Y=4) = \frac{1+1+8}{30} = \frac{10}{30} = \boxed{\frac{1}{3}}.$$

steps.label: Part (c)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (c). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(c)
$$E(Y) = \frac{1(2) + 2(1) + 3(1) + 4(8) + 5(17) + 6(1)}{30} = \frac{2+2+3+32+85+6}{30} = \frac{130}{30} = \boxed{\frac{13}{3}}.$$

steps.label: Part (d)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (d). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(d) $X = 15 - 2Y$, so $Y \le X$ becomes
$$Y \le 15 - 2Y \iff 3Y \le 15 \iff Y \le 5.$$
Hence
$$P(Y \le X) = P(Y \le 5) = 1 - P(Y=6) = 1 - \frac{1}{30} = \boxed{\frac{29}{30}}.$$

steps.label: Part (e)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (e). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(e)
$$E(Y^2) = \frac{1(2) + 4(1) + 9(1) + 16(8) + 25(17) + 36(1)}{30} = \frac{2+4+9+128+425+36}{30} = \frac{604}{30} = \frac{302}{15}.$$
$$\mathrm{Var}(Y) = E(Y^2) - [E(Y)]^2 = \frac{302}{15} - \frac{169}{9}.$$
Common denominator 45:
$$\mathrm{Var}(Y) = \frac{906 - 845}{45} = \frac{61}{45}.$$
Apply $\mathrm{Var}(15 - 2Y) = (-2)^2 \mathrm{Var}(Y) = 4 \mathrm{Var}(Y)$:
$$\mathrm{Var}(X) = \frac{244}{45} \approx \boxed{5.42 \text{ (3 s.f.)}}.$$

steps.label: Audit checklist
steps.body:
Strategy. Use this final box after solving to check formula choice, mark-winning statements, units, and the specific traps from this paper.

Active-recall protocol. Before revealing any work box, say out loud what the visible question is asking for, list the given values with units, and choose the formula or theorem that earns the method mark. Then reveal one box at a time and check that your own line of working has the same structure: setup first, substitution second, simplification third, boxed answer last. If a part depends on a previous result, write that carried result at the top of your scratch work before starting the new part. After the final box, check rounding, units, sign, domain restrictions, and whether the answer is plausible in the context of the question. This card is built so the question header remains visible while every grey work box can be masked separately in Anki Image Occlusion.

Self-test prompts. Can you name the first formula without looking? Can you explain why the denominator, sign, or transformation is the one used here? Can you reproduce the algebra without skipping the intermediate line that would earn the method mark? Can you state the final answer with the exact rounding and context the examiner expects? If any answer is no, re-mask the relevant work box and repeat that part before moving to the next question.

Formula anchor.

Key formula / concept.
$$\sum P(Y=y) = 1,\quad E(Y) = \sum y P(Y=y),\quad \mathrm{Var}(Y) = E(Y^2) - [E(Y)]^2,$$
$$E(aY+b) = aE(Y)+b,\quad \mathrm{Var}(aY+b) = a^2 \mathrm{Var}(Y).$$

Exam checklist before moving on.

Exam checklist.
- Numerical pmf tabulated and summed to 1 before any other computation.
- For (d), inequality manipulated algebraically (3 lines, no enumeration).
- For (e), $\mathrm{Var}$ multiplier squared; constant 15 dropped.
- Final $\mathrm{Var}$ checked positive.

Common traps to actively avoid.

Common pitfalls.
- Putting $-2$ rather than $+4$ as the variance multiplier.
- Forgetting that $X$ is a function of $Y$ (so they are not independent).
- Slipping the $E(Y^2)$ arithmetic --- the term $25(17)=425$ is the easiest place to err.
steps.result: Completion target: answer matches the boxed result and every checklist item is satisfied.

title: S1 S1 May2023 Q6 — Mutually Exclusive, Conditional, Independence, Three-Set Venn
meta: S1 · Q6 · S1 May 2023 (WST01/01, Thu 11 May 2023) · Attempted 12 May 2026
heading: Q6 — Mutually Exclusive, Conditional, Independence, Three-Set Venn
question_html:
Three events $A, B, C$ satisfy $P(A) = 0.1$, $P(B \mid A) = 0.3$, $P(A \cup B) = 0.25$, $P(C) = 0.5$. Given that $A$ and $C$ are mutually exclusive:
(a) Find $P(A \cup C)$. (1)
(b) Show that $P(B) = 0.18$. (3)
Given also that $B$ and $C$ are independent:
(c) Draw a Venn diagram for $A, B, C$ with all region probabilities. (5)

steps.label: Part (a)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (a). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(a)
$$P(A \cup C) = P(A) + P(C) - P(A \cap C) = 0.1 + 0.5 - 0 = \boxed{0.6}.$$

Mistake guard from the attempt/revision notes.

What went wrong.. The classic error: confusing mutually exclusive with independent. Mutually exclusive means $P(A \cap C) = 0$; independent means $P(B \cap C) = P(B)P(C)$. They are different --- in fact, two events with positive probability cannot be both.

Phuc's likely incorrect approach (c).. Setting $P(B \cap C) = 0$ because the prior parts had a mutually-exclusive condition, instead of using the independence of $B$ and $C$ stated for part (c).

steps.label: Part (b)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (b). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(b)
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A)\,P(B \mid A) = 0.1 \times 0.3 = 0.03.$$
Inclusion--exclusion:
$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B) \implies 0.25 = 0.1 + P(B) - 0.03,$$
$$P(B) = 0.25 - 0.1 + 0.03 = \boxed{0.18}. \quad ✓$$

steps.label: Part (c)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (c). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(c) Translate constraints:
- $P(A) = 0.10$, $P(B) = 0.18$, $P(C) = 0.50$.
- $P(A \cap B) = 0.03$.
- $P(A \cap C) = 0$ (mutually exclusive).
- $P(B \cap C) = P(B)P(C) = 0.18 \times 0.5 = 0.09$ (independence).
- $P(A \cap B \cap C) = 0$ --- because $A \cap C = \emptyset$ forces any subset, including $A \cap B \cap C$, to be empty.

Fill from the centre outward:

Region Calculation Probability
$A \cap B \cap C$ forced 0
$A \cap B$ only $0.03 - 0$ 0.03
$A \cap C$ only given 0
$B \cap C$ only $0.09 - 0$ 0.09
$A$ only $0.10 - 0.03 - 0 + 0$ 0.07
$B$ only $0.18 - 0.03 - 0.09 + 0$ 0.06
$C$ only $0.50 - 0 - 0.09 + 0$ 0.41
Outside $A \cup B \cup C$ $1 - (0.07 + 0.06 + 0.41 + 0.03 + 0 + 0.09 + 0)$ 0.34

Total check: $0.07 + 0.06 + 0.41 + 0.03 + 0 + 0.09 + 0 + 0.34 = 1.00$ ✓

Diagram layout: three overlapping circles with $C$ disjoint from $A$ (so $A$ and $C$ should not share any region). $B$ overlaps both $A$ (with 0.03) and $C$ (with 0.09) but the $A \cap B \cap C$ region is empty. Place "0.07" inside $A$ only, "0.41" inside $C$ only, "0.06" inside $B$ only, "0.03" in $A \cap B$ (no $C$), "0.09" in $B \cap C$ (no $A$), and "0.34" outside all three.

steps.label: Audit checklist
steps.body:
Strategy. Use this final box after solving to check formula choice, mark-winning statements, units, and the specific traps from this paper.

Active-recall protocol. Before revealing any work box, say out loud what the visible question is asking for, list the given values with units, and choose the formula or theorem that earns the method mark. Then reveal one box at a time and check that your own line of working has the same structure: setup first, substitution second, simplification third, boxed answer last. If a part depends on a previous result, write that carried result at the top of your scratch work before starting the new part. After the final box, check rounding, units, sign, domain restrictions, and whether the answer is plausible in the context of the question. This card is built so the question header remains visible while every grey work box can be masked separately in Anki Image Occlusion.

Self-test prompts. Can you name the first formula without looking? Can you explain why the denominator, sign, or transformation is the one used here? Can you reproduce the algebra without skipping the intermediate line that would earn the method mark? Can you state the final answer with the exact rounding and context the examiner expects? If any answer is no, re-mask the relevant work box and repeat that part before moving to the next question.

Formula anchor.

Key formula / concept.
$$P(A \cap C) = 0 \text{ (mutually exclusive)},\quad P(B \cap C) = P(B)P(C) \text{ (independent)}.$$
Mutually exclusive $\Rightarrow$ events with positive probability cannot be independent.

Exam checklist before moving on.

Exam checklist.
- All three circles drawn with $A$ and $C$ shown as disjoint.
- Every region (including triple) carries a probability.
- Outside region (0.34) labelled --- it's a 1-mark loss to forget.
- Total probabilities sum to 1 (write the check on your script).
- Mutually-exclusive $\Rightarrow$ triple intersection = 0 explicitly stated.

Common traps to actively avoid.

Common pitfalls.
- Putting $P(B \cap C) = 0$ from confusion with $A \cap C = 0$.
- Negative "only" region from arithmetic slip.
- Omitting the outside region.
steps.result: Completion target: answer matches the boxed result and every checklist item is satisfied.

title: S1 S1 May2023 Q7 — Normal Distribution, Inverse Normal, Two-Equation System
meta: S1 · Q7 · S1 May 2023 (WST01/01, Thu 11 May 2023) · Attempted 12 May 2026
heading: Q7 — Normal Distribution, Inverse Normal, Two-Equation System
question_html:
$J \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ models the volume of juice extracted from 1 kg of apples. With $\mu = 500$, $\sigma = 25$, use standardisation to:
(a)(i) Show that $P(J > 510) = 0.3446$. (2)
(a)(ii) Find $d$ such that $P(J > d) = 0.9192$. (3)
(b) For 5 independent bags, find the probability that each yields less than 510 ml. (2)
After adjustment, $R \sim N(520, k^2)$ with $P(R < r) = 0.15$ and $P(R > 3r - 800) = 0.005$.
(c) Find the values of $r$ and $k$. (7)

steps.label: Setup
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Setup. The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(a)(i)
$$z = \frac{510-500}{25} = 0.4,\qquad P(Z > 0.4) = 1 - \Phi(0.4) = 1 - 0.6554 = \boxed{0.3446}.\ ✓$$

(a)(ii) $P(J > d) = 0.9192 \Rightarrow P(J \le d) = 0.0808$. This is a lower-tail probability < 0.5, so $z < 0$. Solve $\Phi(-z) = 0.9192 \Rightarrow -z = 1.40 \Rightarrow z = -1.40$.
$$d = \mu + z\sigma = 500 + (-1.40)(25) = 500 - 35 = \boxed{465}.$$

Mistake guard from the attempt/revision notes.

What went wrong.. Three predictable errors:
1. In (a)(ii), $P(J > d) = 0.9192$ is more than 0.5, so $d$ lies below the mean $\Rightarrow$ $z$ is negative. Students forgetting the sketch make $z = +1.40$ and end up with $d = 535$.
2. In (b), multiplying $P(J<510)$ by 5 instead of raising to the 5th power.
3. In (c), wrong $z$-value because of confusion between body table $\Phi(z)$ and the percentage-point table.

Phuc's likely incorrect approach (a)(ii).. Looking up $\Phi(z) = 0.9192$ $\Rightarrow$ $z = 1.40$, then writing $d = 500 + 1.40 \times 25 = 535$ --- wrong sign of $z$.

steps.label: Part (b)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (b). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(b)
$$P(J < 510) = 1 - 0.3446 = 0.6554.$$
For 5 independent bags:
$$P(\text{all 5} < 510) = (0.6554)^5 = 0.12095 \approx \boxed{0.121}.$$

steps.label: Part (c)
steps.body:
Strategy. Reveal this box only after you have chosen the method for Part (c). The goal is to reproduce the exam-safe working, not just the final answer.

(c) Translate the two probabilities into $z$-equations.

For $P(R < r) = 0.15$ (lower tail < 0.5 $\Rightarrow$ $z<0$): $\Phi(-z) = 0.85$, percentage-point/body lookup gives $-z \approx 1.0364$, so $z_1 = -1.0364$:
$$\frac{r - 520}{k} = -1.0364 \implies r = 520 - 1.0364k. \tag{$\star$}$$

For $P(R > 3r - 800) = 0.005$ (upper tail < 0.5 $\Rightarrow$ $z>0$): from the percentage-point table, $z_{0.005} = 2.5758$:
$$\frac{(3r - 800) - 520}{k} = 2.5758 \implies \frac{3r - 1320}{k} = 2.5758 \implies 3r = 1320 + 2.5758k$$
$$\implies r = 440 + 0.8586k. \tag{$\star\star$}$$

Equate $(\star)$ and $(\star\star)$:
$$520 - 1.0364k = 440 + 0.8586k \implies 80 = 1.8950k$$
$$\implies k = \frac{80}{1.8950} = 42.2163 \approx \boxed{42.2}.$$

Substitute back:
$$r = 520 - 1.0364 \times 42.2163 = 520 - 43.7531 = 476.2469 \approx \boxed{476}.$$

Check: $3r - 800 = 1428.74 - 800 = 628.74$, $z = (628.74 - 520)/42.22 = 2.5758$ ✓; $z = (476.25 - 520)/42.22 = -1.0364$ ✓.

steps.label: Audit checklist
steps.body:
Strategy. Use this final box after solving to check formula choice, mark-winning statements, units, and the specific traps from this paper.

Active-recall protocol. Before revealing any work box, say out loud what the visible question is asking for, list the given values with units, and choose the formula or theorem that earns the method mark. Then reveal one box at a time and check that your own line of working has the same structure: setup first, substitution second, simplification third, boxed answer last. If a part depends on a previous result, write that carried result at the top of your scratch work before starting the new part. After the final box, check rounding, units, sign, domain restrictions, and whether the answer is plausible in the context of the question. This card is built so the question header remains visible while every grey work box can be masked separately in Anki Image Occlusion.

Self-test prompts. Can you name the first formula without looking? Can you explain why the denominator, sign, or transformation is the one used here? Can you reproduce the algebra without skipping the intermediate line that would earn the method mark? Can you state the final answer with the exact rounding and context the examiner expects? If any answer is no, re-mask the relevant work box and repeat that part before moving to the next question.

Formula anchor.

Key formula / concept.
$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma},\quad Z \sim N(0,1).$$
Percentage-point table values to memorise: $z_{0.05} = 1.6449$, $z_{0.025} = 1.9600$, $z_{0.01} = 2.3263$, $z_{0.005} = 2.5758$.

Exam checklist before moving on.

Exam checklist.
- Sketch of $N(0,1)$ with shaded region drawn before reading any table.
- For "show that": $\Phi$ value quoted from the table to 4 d.p.
- Independence stated explicitly for the "5 bags" step before raising to a power.
- For two-equation systems: both equations written in $z$-form, then both rearranged to $r = \ldots k$ form, then equated.
- Final $k$ checked positive (a negative $\sigma$ is meaningless).
- Verification: substitute $(r, k)$ back into both original probability statements.

Common traps to actively avoid.

Common pitfalls.
- Wrong sign on $z$ in (a)(ii) and in $(\star)$.
- Multiplying instead of exponentiating in (b).
- Using $\Phi(z) = 0.995$ from the body table (rounded $z = 2.58$) when the percentage-point table gives the exact $2.5758$.
- Forgetting that $k$ here plays the role of $\sigma$, not $\sigma^2$.
steps.result: Completion target: answer matches the boxed result and every checklist item is satisfied.
